perturbation.original_text: For a period of 30 days from the time of delivery of the Disposition Notice Glamis will, if requested by Western Silver, entertain an offer from Western Copper to acquire the Designated Mineral Property.
perturbation.changed_text: For a period of 10 days from the time of delivery of the Disposition Notice Glamis will, if requested by Western Silver, entertain an offer from Western Copper to acquire the Designated Mineral Property.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the negotiation period from 30 days to 10 days could be unreasonably short, potentially conflicting with good faith negotiation requirements under contract law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Good Faith Negotiation - Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: Modifying the text from 30 days to 10 days for entertaining an offer may violate the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, especially if 10 days is deemed insufficient to properly assess a Designated Mineral Property and make a reasonable offer. While the Restatement isn't law, it is a persuasive authority and reflects general contract law principles across many jurisdictions.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The original text provided 30 days for Western Copper to make an offer, while the changed text reduces this period to 10 days. The Perturbation Explanation indicates that a negotiation period of 10 days could be unreasonably short, potentially conflicting with good faith negotiation requirements. The Law Explanation reinforces this by stating this may violate the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines 'good faith' as encompassing honest dealing, faithful performance of duties, observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent. By shortening the period to 10 days, it introduces an inconsistency that may violate federal or state law as the new timeline may be insufficient to act in 'good faith', thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Right of First Offer 2.2 Glamis covenants and agrees with Western Copper that if at any time it intends to dispose of a Designated Mineral Property for cash consideration or by abandonment, it will give Western Copper notice (the ' Disposition Notice") of the intended disposition.
perturbation.changed_text: Right of First Offer 2.2 Glamis covenants and agrees with Western Copper that if at any time it intends to dispose of a Designated Mineral Property for cash consideration or by abandonment, it will give Western Copper notice (the ' Disposition Notice") of the intended disposition. Such notice will only be provided if Glamis deems it necessary.
perturbation.explanation: Adding the condition 'Such notice will only be provided if Glamis deems it necessary' undermines the entire purpose of the Right of First Offer, potentially violating good faith requirements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Good Faith Negotiation - Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: Modifying the text to allow Glamis to decide whether or not to provide notice contradicts the purpose of a Right of First Offer, which is to provide the other party (Western Copper) the first chance to buy the asset.  The implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing requires parties to act honestly and reasonably in performing their contractual obligations; giving Glamis sole discretion over notice likely violates that covenant. While the Restatement isn't law, it is a persuasive authority and reflects general contract law principles across many jurisdictions.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The changed text allows Glamis to decide when to provide notice, contradicting the purpose of the Right of First Offer. This undermines Western Copper's opportunity to exercise its right. The alteration potentially violates the implied covenant of good faith, which requires parties to act honestly. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines good faith as encompassing 'honest dealing' and 'observance of fair dealing standards'. By giving Glamis sole discretion over notice, the changed text introduces the possibility of Glamis acting in bad faith, thus contradicting the requirement of good faith.

perturbation.original_text: Western Copper covenants and agrees with Glamis that, for a period of 2 years after the Effective Date, it will not, directly or indirectly, either individually or in partnership or jointly or in conjunction with any Person, which will include being a principal, agent, shareholder, or advisor of such Person or in any other manner whatsoever, (a) carry on or be engaged in Mining Activities, or i 162967.3 -4- (b) advise, lend money to, guarantee the debts or obligations of or permit its name to be used by any Person who carries on or is engaged in Mining Activities, in the Area of Non-Competition.
perturbation.changed_text: Western Copper covenants and agrees with Glamis that, for a period of 5 years after the Effective Date, it will not, directly or indirectly, either individually or in partnership or jointly or in conjunction with any Person, which will include being a principal, agent, shareholder, or advisor of such Person or in any other manner whatsoever, (a) carry on or be engaged in Mining Activities, or i 162967.3 -4- (b) advise, lend money to, guarantee the debts or obligations of or permit its name to be used by any Person who carries on or is engaged in Mining Activities, in the Area of Non-Competition.
perturbation.explanation: Increasing the non-compete period from 2 years to 5 years may be deemed unreasonable in duration, depending on the specific facts and circumstances, and therefore unenforceable under relevant jurisdiction law. Since the contract applies to British Columbia, we will use British Columbia law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Enforceability of Non-Competition Clauses - British Columbia Law
perturbation.law_citation: RBC Dominion Securities Inc. v. Merrill Lynch Canada Inc., 2007 BCCA 430
perturbation.law_explanation: Increasing the non-compete period to 5 years makes it potentially unreasonable and unenforceable under British Columbia law. BC courts scrutinize non-compete clauses, and the 5 year duration might be considered excessive, especially if it unduly restricts Western Copper's ability to earn a livelihood.  The cited case, while fact-specific, illustrates the BC Court of Appeal's approach to assessing the reasonableness of non-competition clauses, taking into account factors such as duration and geographic scope. While there is no definitive legal rule saying that 5 years is always too long, it increases the risk of unenforceability.
perturbation.justification: The change extends the non-compete period from 2 years to 5 years. The Perturbation Explanation states that a 5-year non-compete period may be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable under British Columbia law, creating a legal contradiction. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that BC courts scrutinize non-compete clauses and the extended duration increases the risk of unenforceability. While there is no scraped law snippet to definitively confirm a violation, the explanations provided point to a clear risk of the modified clause being deemed unreasonable and thus unenforceable. This creates a direct legal contradiction because the altered term's enforceability is now seriously questionable.
